findings.title: Email confirmation not sent on idempotent re-deletion (422 path)
findings.why_it_matters: When a user calls delete-account a second time and gets the 422 early-return path (alreadyScheduled: true), the pending_deletions row was already upserted (good) but the deletion-scheduled email is not sent again. If the first attempt failed to send the email (e.g., EMAIL_API_KEY not set at that time, now it is), the user never receives the email. This is a corner case but could cause GDPR notice gaps.
evidence: Lines 143-150: early return `json({ success: true, scheduledDeletionAt, alreadyScheduled: true })` is reached before the sendEmail call at line 163
The email send is only attempted after the soft-delete succeeds, not on the 422 idempotent path

findings.title: Deletion probe silently skips tables with missing user_id column
findings.why_it_matters: If a table in TABLE_NAMES does not have a user_id column (schema mismatch or table renamed), the probe logs a WARN and continues — it does not fail. An attacker who can rename a column could prevent the probe from detecting surviving data. More practically, a legitimate schema refactor could silently break the probe.
findings.suggested_fix: Track tables that returned errors separately from tables that are clean. Report both in the summary. Consider: if 3+ tables error, exit 1 as a signal that the probe configuration may be broken.
evidence: Line 79-81: `if (error) { console.warn(...); continue }` — error silently skips the table
A probe that passes because it errored on all tables is indistinguishable from a probe that passed because all tables were clean

residual_risks: cancel link in email routes to /settings which requires the user to be logged in — if the soft-delete made the account inaccessible, the user cannot cancel via the email link
testing_gaps: No test for the adversarial case where probe-constants diverges from hardDeleteUser — would catch GDPR gap silently
